explanation:
Step 1: Original statement in logical form

$ \forall M > 0 \; \exists x \in S \; (x \geq M). $

Step 2: Negation

Negation of $\forall M > 0$ is $\exists M > 0$.

Negation of $\exists x \in S \; (x \geq M)$ is $\forall x \in S \; (x < M)$.

So the negation is:

$ \exists M > 0 \; \forall x \in S \; (x < M). $

Step 3: Match with options

$ \boxed{\text{Option C}} $

$$ =[(q \wedge r \wedge \sim p) \vee F] \vee(p \wedge r) $$

$=(\mathrm{q} \wedge \mathrm{r} \wedge \sim \mathrm{p}) \vee(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{r}) \quad \ldots[$ Identity Law]

content: If the statement pattern $(p \wedge q) \rightarrow(r \vee \sim s)$ is false, then the truth values of $p, q, r$ and $s$ are respectively
explanation:
The statement is $ (p \wedge q) \rightarrow (r \vee \sim s) $.

We are told that this statement is false.

Step 1: Recall Truth Table for Implication ($\rightarrow$)

An implication $A \rightarrow B$ is false only when $A$ is true and $B$ is false.

So,

  • $p \wedge q$ must be true

  • $r \vee \sim s$ must be false

Step 2: Analyze $p \wedge q$

  • $p \wedge q$ is true only if both $p$ and $q$ are true.

  • Therefore, $p = T$ and $q = T$.

Step 3: Analyze $r \vee \sim s$

  • $r \vee \sim s$ is false only if both $r$ is false and $\sim s$ is false

  • $\sim s$ is false $\implies s$ is true

  • So, $r = F$ and $s = T$

Step 4: Write the truth values in order:

$p, q, r, s = T, T, F, T$

Correct Option: D

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{q}: l=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}, \mathrm{~m} & =\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}}, \mathrm{n}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ l^2+\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2 & =\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{4}{3}+\frac{4}{3}+\frac{1}{3} \\ & =3 \neq 1 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a class of 300 students, every student reads 5 news papers and every news paper is read by 60 students. Then the number of newspapers is
explanation:
To find the number of newspapers, we can use the relationship between the total number of readings, the number of students, the number of newspapers, and the conditions given:

Each of the 300 students reads 5 newspapers, which results in:

$$ \text{Total Readings} = 300 \times 5 = 1500 $$

Each newspaper is read by 60 students. Let $ n $ be the number of newspapers, thus:

$$ \text{Total Readings} = n \times 60 $$

Setting these two expressions for total readings equal, we have:

$$ 300 \times 5 = n \times 60 $$

Simplifying the equation:

$$ 1500 = 60n $$

Dividing both sides by 60:

$$ n = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 $$

Thus, the number of newspapers is exactly 25.

Option C: exactly 25 is the correct answer.

content: If p and q are statements, then _________ is a contingency.
explanation:
A contingency is a proposition (compound statement) that is neither a tautology (always true) nor a contradiction (always false). Let us check each option:

$\mathrm{p} \wedge \lnot \mathrm{p}$

Always false (contradiction).

$\mathrm{p} \vee \lnot \mathrm{p}$

Always true (tautology).

$\mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}$

Can be true or false depending on the truth values of $\mathrm{p}$ and $\mathrm{q}$.

If both $\mathrm{p}$ and $\mathrm{q}$ are false, then $\mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}$ is false.

Otherwise, it is true.

Hence it is not always true and not always false; so it is a contingency.

$\bigl(\mathrm{p} \wedge (\mathrm{p} \to \mathrm{q})\bigr) \to \mathrm{q}$

This is actually a tautology (always true). One way to see this is that

$\mathrm{p} \wedge (\mathrm{p} \to \mathrm{q})$ essentially simplifies to $\mathrm{p} \wedge (\lnot \mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q})$,

which is the same as $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \lnot \mathrm{p}) \vee (\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q})$ = $ \text{False} \vee (\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q})$ = $\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q}$. Thus the whole statement is $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q}) \to \mathrm{q}$, which is always true.

Therefore, the contingency is:

(C) $\mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}$.

content: If $$p: \forall n \in I N, n^2+n$$ is an even number $$q: \forall n \in I N, n^2-n$$ is an odd numer, then the truth values of $$p \wedge q, p \vee q$$ and $$p \rightarrow q$$ are respectively
explanation:
Let's analyze each of the propositions given, starting with $$p$$ which states that for every natural number $$n$$, the expression $$n^2 + n$$ is even.

The expression $$n^2 + n$$ can be factored into $$n(n + 1)$$. Since any integer $$n$$ is either even or odd, if $$n$$ is even, $$n(n + 1)$$ will certainly be even because an even number times any other number is even. If $$n$$ is odd, then $$n + 1$$ is even, and again, an odd number times an even number is also even. Thus, in either case, the expression will be even, and $$p$$ is true.

Now let's look at $$q$$, which posits that for every natural number $$n$$, the expression $$n^2 - n$$ is odd.

Similarly, we can factor the expression into $$n(n - 1)$$, which is the product of two consecutive numbers. Just as in the first case, if $$n$$ is even, $$n - 1$$ is odd, resulting in an even number. If $$n$$ is odd, then $$n - 1$$ is even, which again results in an even number. Thus, $$q$$ is false because the product of two consecutive integers is always even.

Therefore, the truth value of $$p$$ is True (T), and the truth value of $$q$$ is False (F).

Next, we'll find the truth values of the compound statements:

  • $$p \wedge q$$ is the conjunction of $$p$$ and $$q$$, and it is true if and only if both $$p$$ and $$q$$ are true. Since $$q$$ is false, $$p \wedge q$$ is false (F).

  • $$p \vee q$$ is the disjunction of $$p$$ and $$q$$, and it is true if at least one of $$p$$ or $$q$$ is true. Since $$p$$ is true, $$p \vee q$$ is true (T).

  • $$p \rightarrow q$$ is the conditional statement "if $$p$$ then $$q$$" and is only false if $$p$$ is true and $$q$$ is false (since the only way for an implication to be false is if a true premise leads to a false conclusion). Since this is precisely the case here, $$p \rightarrow q$$ is false (F).

So the correct truth values for $$p \wedge q$$, $$p \vee q$$, and $$p \rightarrow q$$ are respectively False (F), True (T), and False (F).

The right option based on the above reasoning is:

Option A $$F, T, F$$.

content: The negation of the statement, "The payment will be made if and only if the work is finished in time" is

options.content: The work is finished in time and the payment is not made or the payment is made and the work is finished in time.
The work is finished in time and the payment is not made.
The payment is made and the work is not finished in time.
Either the work is finished in time and the payment is not made or the payment is made and the work is not finished in time.

explanation:
The given statement is a biconditional statement, which has the form:

$$ P \Leftrightarrow Q $$

where $ P $ is "the work is finished in time," and $ Q $ is "the payment will be made."

In propositional logic, the negation of a biconditional statement $ P \Leftrightarrow Q $ is:

$$ \neg(P \Leftrightarrow Q) $$

Which is logically equivalent to:

$$ (P \land \neg Q) \lor (\neg P \land Q) $$

That is, for the negation of "if and only if," one part must be true while the other part must be false.

So, for the negation of the statement "The payment will be made if and only if the work is finished in time," we can interpret the negated statement as either:

1. The work is finished in time and the payment is not made. (This corresponds to $ P \land \neg Q $).

2. The payment is made and the work is not finished in time. (This corresponds to $ \neg P \land Q $).

Therefore, the negation of the statement would mean that one of these two possibilities must occur – either the work is finished on time and payment is not made, or the payment is made but the work is not finished on time.

Hence, the correct negation of the given statement is:

Option D

"Either the work is finished in time and the payment is not made or the payment is made and the work is not finished in time."

explanation:
To determine the negation of the statement "$$\forall x \in N, x^2 + x$$ is an even number," we first need to understand what the original statement means.

The statement "$$\forall x \in N, x^2 + x$$ is an even number" means that for every natural number $$x$$, the expression $$x^2 + x$$ results in an even number.

The negation of a universally quantified statement $$\forall x \, P(x)$$ is an existentially quantified statement $$\exists x \, \neg P(x)$$. In other words, if the original statement asserts that a property holds for all elements in a set, its negation asserts that there is at least one element in the set for which the property does not hold.

Therefore, the negation of "$$\forall x \in N, x^2 + x$$ is an even number" is:

$$\exists x \in N$$ such that $$x^2 + x$$ is not an even number.

So, the correct option is:

Option D $$\exists x \in N$$ such that $$x^2+x$$ is not an even number.

$$ \neg(\neg p \lor \neg q) $$

Next, we apply De Morgan's laws to simplify $$ \neg(\neg p \lor \neg q) $$, which gives us:

content: The expression $$[(p \wedge \sim q) \vee q] \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$$ is equivalent to
explanation:
Let's break down the given logical expression and simplify it to determine which option it is equivalent to.

We'll use the following logical equivalences:

  • Distributive Law: $$(p \vee q) \wedge r \equiv (p \wedge r) \vee (q \wedge r)$$ and $$(p \wedge q) \vee r \equiv (p \vee r) \wedge (q \vee r)$$
  • Identity Law: $$p \vee \text{False} \equiv p$$ and $$p \wedge \text{True} \equiv p$$
  • Complement Law: $$p \vee \sim p \equiv \text{True}$$ and $$p \wedge \sim p \equiv \text{False}$$
  • De Morgan's Law: $$\sim (p \wedge q) \equiv \sim p \vee \sim q$$ and $$\sim (p \vee q) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim q$$

Now, let's simplify the given expression:

$$[(p \wedge \sim q) \vee q] \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$$

Using the Distributive Law, we can rewrite the first part:

$$[(p \vee q) \wedge (\sim q \vee q)] \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$$

Applying the Complement Law, $$(\sim q \vee q) \equiv \text{True}$$

$$[(p \vee q) \wedge \text{True}] \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$$

Using the Identity Law, $$(p \vee q) \wedge \text{True} \equiv (p \vee q)$$

$$(p \vee q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$$

Again, using the Distributive Law:

$$[(p \vee \sim p) \wedge (q \vee \sim p)] \vee q$$

Applying the Complement Law, $$(p \vee \sim p) \equiv \text{True}$$

$$[\text{True} \wedge (q \vee \sim p)] \vee q$$

Using the Identity Law, $$\text{True} \wedge (q \vee \sim p) \equiv (q \vee \sim p)$$

$$(q \vee \sim p) \vee q$$

Finally, using the Idempotent Law, $$(q \vee \sim p) \vee q \equiv (q \vee \sim p)$$

Therefore, the simplified expression is $$p \vee q$$.

So the answer is Option A: $$p \vee q$$

content: Negation of the statement $$\forall x \in R, x^2+1=0$$ is
explanation:
To find the negation of the given statement, we start by understanding the original statement:

$$\forall x \in R, x^2+1=0$$

This statement declares that for every real number \(x\), the equation \(x^2+1=0\) holds true. The negation of a statement changes its truth value, essentially turning a universally quantified statement (one that applies to all members of a set) into an existentially quantified statement (one that applies to at least one member of the set), and vice versa. Additionally, it inverts the condition within the statement.

To negate the given statement, we apply the following steps:

  • Change the universal quantifier (\(\forall\)) to an existential quantifier (\(\exists\)).
  • Invert the condition \(x^2+1=0\) to \(x^2+1 \neq 0\). The condition \(x^2+1=0\) being true for all \(x\) in \(R\) is the original claim, so negation requires that there's at least one \(x\) in \(R\) for which that condition doesn't hold.

Therefore, the negation is: "There exists at least one real number \(x\) for which \(x^2+1 \neq 0\)."

This means Option B is the correct negation:

$$\exists x \in R$$ such that $$x^2+1 \neq 0$$.

Options A and C introduce inequalities that were not part of the original statement and thus are incorrect. Option D repeats the original statement, which is not a negation.
